We do not know what a day may bring. This does not forbid preparing for tomorrow, but presuming upon tomorrow. We must not put off the great work of conversion, that one thing needful.
There may be occasion for us to justify ourselves, but not to praise ourselves.
Those who have no control over their passions sink under the load.
Plain and faithful rebukes are better, not only than secret hatred, but than love which compliments in sin, to the hurt of the soul.

The poor have a better appreciation of their enjoyments, and are often more thankful for them, than the rich. In like manner, the proud and self-sufficient disdain the gospel; but those who hunger and thirst after righteousness find comfort from the simplest book or sermon that testifies of Christ Jesus.
Every person has his proper place in society, where he may be safe and comfortable.

Do not depend for relief upon a relative, merely for being related; apply to those who are at hand and will help in need. But there is a Friend that sticks closer than a brother, and let us place entire confidence in him.
An affectionate parent urges his son to prudent conduct that should gladden his heart. The good conduct of Christians is the best answer to all who find fault with the gospel.
Where there is temptation, if we put ourselves into it, there will be sin, and punishment will follow.
An honest person may become a beggar, but he is not honest who makes himself one.
It is foolish to be fond of being praised; it is a temptation to pride.
The contentions of a neighbor may be like a sharp shower, troublesome for a time; the contentions of a wife are like constant rain.
We are cautioned to take heed whom we converse with and directed to have in view, in conversation, to make one another wiser and better.
Though a calling is laborious and despised, those who keep to it will find there is something to be gained by it. God is a Master who has promised to honor those who serve him faithfully.

One corrupt heart is like another; so are sanctified hearts: the former bears the same image of the earthly, the latter the same image of the heavenly. Let us carefully watch our own hearts, comparing them with the word of God.

Two things are said to be never satisfied: death and sin. The appetites of the carnal mind for profit or pleasure are always desiring more. Those whose eyes are ever toward the Lord are satisfied in him, and shall forever be so.
Silver and gold are tried by putting them into a furnace and refining pot; so is a person tried by praising him.
Some are so bad that even severe methods do not answer the end; what remains but that they should be rejected? The new-creating power of God’s grace alone is able to make a change.

We ought to have some business to do in this world, and not to live in idleness, and not to meddle with what we do not understand. We must be diligent and take pains. Let us do what we can; still, the world cannot be secured to us, therefore we must choose a more lasting portion; but by the blessing of God upon our honest labors, we may expect to enjoy as much of earthly blessings as is good for us.
